title: Listening - Part 4: Restoring Historic Boats
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Leah, a wooden-boat restorer. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m with Leah Morgan, who restores old wooden boats at a harbour workshop. Leah, how did boats first become important to you? || Leah: My family were not sailors, so people are surprised that I chose this. While studying design, I worked one summer in a harbour café. Opposite the café was a damaged fishing boat that nobody used. [24]Watching a small team gradually bring that abandoned boat back to life made me want to learn the craft myself[/24]. I loved the idea that repair could preserve local history. || Presenter: What did you find difficult as an apprentice? || Leah: I expected tools to be the challenge, and of course learning to use them safely mattered. But the real skill came before cutting any wood. [25]I had to learn to examine an old boat patiently and understand why a part had failed before deciding how to replace it[/25]. Otherwise, you simply hide a problem and it returns later. || Presenter: Do you always use traditional materials? || Leah: We want boats to look and behave as their original builders intended, but they must also be safe to use. For instance, old fastenings can weaken invisibly. [26]We sometimes use modern materials in places passengers cannot see when this makes a restored boat safer and longer lasting[/26]. Preservation is not about pretending we still live a century ago. || Presenter: What makes restoration especially challenging now? || Leah: Finding wood of the right type and quality. Historic boats were often built from large, slow-grown trees, and such timber is harder to obtain responsibly today. [27]We may spend months looking for suitable wood rather than use the wrong material simply to finish quickly[/27]. Owners have to understand why that delay protects their boat. || Presenter: Volunteers often visit the workshop. Does that slow you down? || Leah: A new volunteer needs supervision, so some jobs take longer. Yet local people bring knowledge that no manual contains. [28]Several have shown us family photographs revealing details of boats before later repairs changed their appearance[/28]. Those images help us restore colours or fittings correctly, so the relationship works both ways. || Presenter: What is it like when a completed boat returns to the water? || Leah: People expect me to say I feel proud, and I do eventually. At first I am watching every join and listening for unexpected sounds. [29]The strongest feeling at a launch is relief when the boat floats securely and handles as planned[/29]. Only then can I stand back and admire all those months of careful work. || Presenter: What qualities does a boat restorer need? || Leah: Curiosity, practical ability and respect for other craftspeople are useful. Above all, [30]you must be comfortable with progress that is slow and sometimes almost invisible from day to day[/30]. Sanding a surface or fitting a single piece exactly may not look exciting, but those quiet stages determine whether the boat lasts.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first interested Leah in boat restoration?

options.A: Seeing an unused boat being repaired.
options.B: Learning to sail with her family.
options.C: Designing a boat while at college.

questions.explanation: She became interested while watching a team restore a damaged fishing boat.

questions.question: What important skill did Leah learn as an apprentice?

options.A: Choosing tools for different kinds of wood.
options.B: Finding the cause of damage before repairing it.
options.C: Working quickly when a boat owner is waiting.

questions.explanation: She had to understand why a part failed before replacing it.

questions.question: Why does Leah sometimes use modern materials?

options.A: To improve safety without changing visible character.
options.B: To make boats look more attractive to buyers.
options.C: To reduce the need for skilled work.

questions.explanation: She uses them in unseen places where they make boats safer and longer lasting.

questions.question: What can delay a restoration project?
questions.explanation: Suitable, responsibly obtained wood can take months to find.

questions.question: How can volunteers help Leah's work?

options.C: By finding owners for old boats.

questions.explanation: Family photographs have shown how boats appeared before later repairs.

questions.question: How does Leah initially feel when a boat is launched?

options.A: Disappointed that the project is over.
options.B: Relieved that the boat performs safely.

questions.explanation: Her strongest first feeling is relief when it floats and handles correctly.

questions.question: According to Leah, what is particularly necessary in her profession?

options.A: The patience to accept gradual progress.
options.B: The ability to draw original boat designs.
options.C: The confidence to work entirely alone.

questions.explanation: Restorers must be comfortable with slow, sometimes almost invisible progress.
